word_list.pronunciation: tˈeɪk kˈaɪndli tʊ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to show that one is attracted by or pleased with someone or something

examples.x: The teacher took kindly to the student's sincere apology and offered guidance for improvement.
I don't think she'll take kindly to your criticism of her work.
The company took kindly to the customer's feedback and promptly addressed their concerns.
I know the people of these lands enough to tell you that they won't take kindly to outsiders.
The community took kindly to the new park, and it quickly became a popular gathering spot.

senses.definition: used to show that someone has made a good suggestion or guess

examples.x: You bet!
You bet, I've got you covered.

senses.definition: shared opinions, beliefs, or interests between parties that have disagreements about other things

examples.x: Despite their differing political views, the panelists were able to find common ground on the importance of economic stability.
The negotiation process aimed to identify common ground between the two parties to reach a mutually beneficial agreement.
The team-building exercise encouraged employees to explore their shared values and find common ground for effective collaboration.
The therapy session focused on finding common ground between the couple to improve their communication and resolve conflicts.
A successful debate requires finding common ground with the audience, appealing to their shared experiences and values.

senses.definition: used to acknowledge the validity or logic behind someone's assertion, even if one doesn't entirely agree with it

examples.x: Fair enough, I'll make sure to spend more time on my academics.
Fair enough, everyone has their own preferences when it comes to movies.
Fair enough, let's go with your color choice.
Fair enough, financial responsibility is important.

senses.definition: used to tell someone that one completely understands or agrees with what they are saying

examples.x: I hear you, it can be exhausting.
I hear you, exams can be stressful.
I hear you, environmental consciousness is important.

senses.definition: used to express one's complete agreement with someone's statement

examples.x: You can say that again!
The test was difficult—oh, you can say that again!
You can say that again cause I feel like I'm melting.
If she wins again, you can say that again—she's unbeatable.

senses.definition: to completely agree with someone and understand their point of view

examples.x: Despite their different backgrounds, the two politicians were able to see eye to eye on the need for educational reform.
The siblings rarely see eye to eye on matters of fashion, often having contrasting styles and preferences.
The negotiation process was challenging, but eventually, both parties found common ground and began to see eye to eye.
The team members had different approaches to problem-solving, but through open communication, they managed to see eye to eye and reach effective solutions.
It took some time for the new colleagues to understand each other's perspectives, but eventually, they began to see eye to eye and work collaboratively.

senses.definition: to attempt to make people accept ideas, opinions, etc. that they already agree with

examples.x: The political candidate's speech at the rally was well-received, but some argued that it was preaching to the converted since the attendees already supported their campaign.
The professor's lecture on the benefits of exercise in a sports science class seemed like preaching to the choir, as the students were already knowledgeable on the topic.
The author's book on the importance of education received praise, but some critics felt that it was preaching to the choir, as its readers were likely already aware of the value of education.
The speaker passionately advocated for stricter gun control laws during the conference, but most of the attendees were already supporters, so it felt like preaching to the choir.

The environmental activist gave a powerful speech about the urgency of combating climate change, but the event was attended mostly by environmentalists, making it feel like preaching to the converted.

senses.definition: used to say that one person has the same ideas, opinions, or mentality as another person

examples.x: During the brainstorming session, the team members quickly realized they were on the same wavelength, generating innovative ideas seamlessly.
The couple, having been together for years, often finished each other's sentences, demonstrating their ability to be on the same wavelength.
The teacher and student had a productive discussion because they were on the same wavelength regarding the educational goals and strategies.
The comedians effortlessly built upon each other's jokes during the improvisational performance, showcasing their ability to be on the same wavelength humorously.
In the business meeting, the executives found it easy to make decisions as they were on the same wavelength regarding the company's vision and objectives.

senses.definition: in complete agreement with someone

examples.x: The coach gathered the team before the game to ensure they were on the same page regarding the game plan and tactics.
The family meeting clarified expectations and ensured that all family members were on the same page regarding household responsibilities.
The CEO addressed the company's employees to ensure everyone was on the same page regarding the new strategic direction.
The couple discussed their vacation plans and made sure they were on the same page about the destination and travel dates.
The project team had a productive meeting and ensured everyone was on the same page regarding the project timeline and objectives.

senses.definition: a situation in which two parties are in full agreement with one another

examples.x: The negotiations were successful as both parties experienced a meeting of minds on the key terms of the contract.
The panel discussion fostered a meeting of minds among the experts, leading to insightful conversations and the exploration of new perspectives.
The brainstorming session encouraged a meeting of the minds within the team, generating innovative ideas and solutions.
The book club members experienced a meeting of minds during their lively discussion, as they all appreciated the intricate themes and symbolism within the novel.
The workshop aimed to facilitate a meeting of minds among participants from diverse backgrounds, encouraging the exchange of ideas and fostering mutual understanding.

senses.definition: an instance or gesture that indicates approval or satisfaction

examples.x: After tasting the dish, the food critic gave it a thumbs up, recommending it to their readers.
The teacher gave the students a thumbs up for their excellent presentations, acknowledging their hard work and preparation.
The audience responded with a thumbs up when the speaker made a compelling argument, expressing agreement and satisfaction.

senses.definition: used to express one's agreement or approval
to say or do the exact right thing in a particular situation

examples.x: The coach analyzed my form and said I was holding the racket too tight - he really hit the nail on the head with that observation.
The detective hit the nail on the head when he suspected it was the jealous ex-spouse behind the crime.
In her speech, the politician hit the nail on the head about the real issues affecting voters.
My friend's advice to enjoy life to the fullest really hit the nail on the head about making the most of time.
The movie's depiction of office politics hit the nail on the head from my own experience.

senses.definition: to seem true or reasonable to one

examples.x: His explanation for failing the test didn't really ring true to me.
The witness' report of events that night just didn't ring true with the evidence found.
The statement from the politician rings true with how I've seen her govern in the past.
Hearing how hard they've worked to succeed in business really rings true and is inspirational.
Studies show exercise reduces stress, and from experience that sure rings true for me.

senses.definition: to support someone who will later bring one profit by achieving success

examples.x: By investing in renewable technology early, that company really backed the right horse for future growth.
It seems like the new candidate has the best chance of winning, so I'm going to back the right horse and vote for her.
I'm confident this treatment plan will work, so as her doctor I'm backing the right horse and recommending she try it.
By choosing the reputable contractor for the home renovation, I think we backed the right horse for a high-quality job.

word_list.word: it would not do {sb} any harm
word_list.pronunciation: ɪt wˈʊdənt dˈuː sˈʌmwʌn ˌɛni hˈɑːɹm

senses.definition: used to refer to something as a good idea or a reasonable choice
to take back something one has said previously

examples.x: I said I'd never try skydiving, but after loving it I had to eat my words.
The weather forecaster had to eat their words when the storm they predicted failed to arrive.
After the election upset, the pundits who claimed the outcome was decided had to eat their words.
When my investment paid off against all odds, my naysaying friend ate their words.
You doubted I could lose 20 pounds - I think it's time for you to eat your words!

word_list.word: that is about the size of it
word_list.pronunciation: ðæt ɪz ɐbˌaʊt ðə sˈaɪz ʌv ɪt

senses.definition: used to emphasize the truth of what one is saying

examples.x: After explaining the issue, my friend said "that is about the size of it" in agreeing I pinpointed the problem.
Budget cuts and low morale - that is about the size of it when it comes to why productivity is down.
A shortage of materials coupled with delays - that is about the size of it for the construction problems.
Unrealistic deadlines and too much work - that is about the size of it for why I've been so stressed.
A failure to connect and long hours apart - that is about the size of it for what caused their marriage to break down.

senses.definition: to sign a document to show that one has agreed to do or buy something

examples.x: The entrepreneur was excited about the business deal and eagerly signed on the dotted line, officially sealing the partnership.
The athlete carefully reviewed the sponsorship contract before confidently signing on the dotted line, securing their endorsement agreement.
The homebuyer carefully read through the mortgage agreement and sought legal advice before signing on the dotted line.
After negotiating the terms of the employment contract, the candidate was ready to sign on the dotted line and accept the job offer.
The artist was overjoyed when the gallery offered them a representation agreement, and they wasted no time in signing on the dotted line.

word_list.pronunciation: sˈɪt wˈɛl ɹˈaɪt wɪð ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to be agreeable or acceptable to someone or something

examples.x: The changes to the project didn't sit well with the team, so they decided to revise the plan.
The idea of implementing this new policy doesn't sit right with our company culture.
The idea of taking credit for someone else's work didn't sit right with me because it goes against my values.
The new policy sits well with the company's values.
